name: Master of Urban Design (MUD)

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The six referenced courses form a coherent post-professional urban-design set: two design studios (Urban Design Studio I and II), History and Theory of Urban Planning and Design, Public and Private Development, GIS, and Topics of Sustainable Urbanism, spanning design, theory, real-estate economics, spatial tools, and sustainability. The set is well-balanced for a focused MUD; the coherence is undercut only by the two studios not being prerequisite-linked, which hides the intended I-to-II progression.
dimensions.suggestion: Encode Urban Design Studio I as a prerequisite for Studio II and publish a short plan of study so the intended year-long studio arc is explicit.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 0 and no course lists a prerequisite, so the graph is entirely flat even though Studio I logically precedes Studio II and Public and Private Development notes 'completion of core sequence' in its description. missingCourseCount is 0, so there are no dangling references, but the absence of any encoded sequencing is a modest structural weakness.
dimensions.suggestion: Add the Studio I to Studio II prerequisite and formalize the 'completion of core sequence' gate on ARCH 651 so the sequencing described in prose is captured in the prerequisite data.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are stated, so the specific urban-design competencies a graduate should demonstrate are left implicit.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish program learning outcomes covering urban-design synthesis, development feasibility, and spatial analysis, mapped to the studios and GIS course.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null and no plan groups were parsed, so the total (a MUD is typically ~30-36 credits, with the two 6-credit studios alone contributing 12) and the core/elective split are not transparent; creditReconcileDelta is uncomputable.
dimensions.suggestion: State total credits and show how the two 6-credit studios plus the 3-credit seminars sum to the degree total.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The six courses are described clearly and specifically, and the titles convey a legible urban-design concentration, but the page lacks a plan of study, total credits, and labeled terms.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a compact plan-of-study table with a credit summary to complement the strong, concise course list.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is notably current: GIS provides spatial-analysis skills, Public and Private Development covers real-estate finance and feasibility, and Topics of Sustainable Urbanism directly addresses cities' ~70% share of carbon emissions and resource use. This aligns well with contemporary urban-design practice.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a mobility/transportation-planning and an urban-analytics elective to cover two areas practice increasingly demands.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and informative, e.g., Sustainable Urbanism opens with concrete data on urban carbon emissions and resource use, and Public and Private Development enumerates needs assessment and real-estate appraisal; none read as placeholder.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: All prerequisite fields are null so entries are unambiguous and free of dangling references, but the studios and the 'completion of core sequence' expectation in ARCH 651 are not encoded, so the stated clarity comes at the cost of missing real sequencing.

missingTopics.rationale: A MUD that covers development economics and GIS but no mobility/transit course omits a core determinant of urban form; a transportation-and-urban-form seminar would fill it.
Public and Private Development addresses real-estate economics but not housing policy or affordability, central concerns of contemporary urban design.
Sustainable Urbanism addresses mitigation and resource use, but adaptation to flooding and heat (resilience planning) is a distinct, missing competency.
No course covers participatory process or equitable engagement, essential to legitimate urban-design practice.

missingTopics.topic: Urban analytics and spatial data science
missingTopics.rationale: GIS provides a foundation, but quantitative spatial analysis and data-driven decision-making are increasingly expected beyond introductory GIS.

emergingTrends.rationale: Green/blue infrastructure and resilience are reshaping urban design; the Sustainable Urbanism course is a natural home to expand toward adaptation and nature-based solutions.
City-scale modeling and digital twins are emerging planning instruments that the GIS course could evolve toward.
Proximity-based, mixed-use models are a leading contemporary paradigm the studios should engage.
Spatial-justice frameworks are increasingly central to urban design and would strengthen the theory and studio components.
Public and Private Development can modernize toward analytics-driven feasibility and market modeling now standard in development practice.

topSuggestions: State total credits and a plan of study showing how the two 6-credit studios and 3-credit seminars sum to the degree.
Encode the Studio I to Studio II prerequisite and the ARCH 651 core-sequence gate so real sequencing is captured.
Publish program learning outcomes for urban-design synthesis, development feasibility, and spatial analysis.
Add transportation/mobility and community-engagement electives to close the most conspicuous topical gaps.
Expand the sustainability strand toward climate-resilience and nature-based-solutions content.
